response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
The topic of the gender gap in science has been bothering me for a while. You see, growing up as a woman with only my dad who had a PhD to influence me, I learned early on that there are certain doors not meant for us girls unless you break through them. My dad was always reading and talking about his research, but he never let me forget how tough it is out there without the right connections or support.

I've been unemployed since my divorce, so I spend most of my time these days on social media scrolling through updates from people like me who are navigating life after being laid off. That's when I first really saw statistics about women in STEM fields and felt that familiar pang of injustice mixed with anxiety—what if our society misses out on brilliant minds just because they're female? It makes no sense to me, especially since my openness score is pretty high at 95; I love seeing potential connections between ideas.

But then again, I'm not a scientist myself anymore. My education only went up to lower secondary, and now with two kids growing up in New York City, there's no way we're moving back anytime soon anyway—my dad says he wants to see how they turn out first.

I think the gender gap is real; it affects opportunities right across every level from hiring committees down through tenure decisions. It shouldn't be addressed only by bringing awareness though—that seems too superficial for what I'm dealing with on a daily basis looking at these issues online. We need systemic change, mentorship programs specifically designed to support women in academia starting early if possible, and equal pay policies enforced rigorously.

I believe closing this gap will bring diversity of thought unparalleled by any other means.
citations: Statistical data from national science boards
Interviews with female scientists on social media
